$comment: Effects available to the design loop. Adding an effect is a data edit here plus a folder — the skill reads this file and needs no changes.

effects.name: Marble Ink (Japanese)
effects.summary: Animated mesh gradient — domain-warped fBm noise with a cursor-driven force field. Reads as ink floated on water.
gateRisks: contrast: text over the gradient can drop below 4.5:1 on bright frames — a scrim is mandatory, not optional
overflow: canvas must be position:fixed/absolute inset-0, never a sized block in flow
pointer-events: canvas must be pointer-events:none or it eats every click beneath it

console: over file:// the origin is null and ES-module imports are CORS-blocked — prototypes MUST use the `global` build, not `entry`. Verified: <script type=module> fails the gate, <script src> passes.
effects.cost: one fullscreen triangle, 5-octave fBm x3 per pixel; dprCap 1.5 and autoPause on by default

effects.name: Kinetic Type
effects.summary: Text-as-motion for headlines: per-unit rise (fade+lift+de-blur), scramble/decode, or a gentle drift. A different category from a background field — the type itself is the effect.
gateRisks: a11y: the real text is kept as the element's aria-label and the split spans are aria-hidden, so a screen reader hears the word not the scrambling glyphs. Built in — do not strip it.
reduced-motion: respectReducedMotion default true snaps to the final text with no motion. The `wave` mode is the one that runs continuously; never disable the snap.
layout: each unit is inline-block, which can change where a line breaks. Apply to a heading with room, not a width-constrained label.

motion: uses the Web Animations API, not CSS transitions, so it is invisible to CSS-only motion checks AND never blocks interaction. A cold auditor should still eyeball that the entrance is not sluggish.
console: use the `global` build over file:// (classic <script src>), never an ES-module import.
effects.cost: one WAAPI animation per unit for the one-shot entrance; `wave` keeps N infinite animations running, so use it sparingly and never on a long paragraph
